---
title: "A first Regression with PyFixest"
aliases:
  - /quickstart.html
format:
  html:
    html-table-processing: none
toc: true
toc-title: "On this page"
toc-location: left
---

`PyFixest` is a Python package for fast high-dimensional fixed effects regression.

The package aims to mimic the syntax and functionality of [Laurent Bergé's](https://sites.google.com/site/laurentrberge/) formidable [fixest](https://github.com/lrberge/fixest) package as closely as Python allows: if you know `fixest` well, the goal is that you won't have to read the docs to get started! In particular, this means that all of `fixest's` defaults are mirrored by `PyFixest`.

## Installation

You can install `pyfixest` from [PyPi](https://pypi.org/project/pyfixest/) https://pypi.org/project/pyfixest/ via 

```bash
pip install -U pyfixest
```

## A First Regression

We want to estimate the **causal returns of education on earnings** via a twin study. In this notebook, we focus on `PyFixest` estimation functionality and syntax. For details on the question at hand, please take a look at the [OLS with Fixed Effects](tutorials/ols-fixed-effects.qmd) vignette.

In a first step, we load a twin-study style data set: 

```{python}
import pyfixest as pf

twins = pf.get_twin_data(N_pairs=500, seed=42)
twins.head()
```

`PyFixest`'s core estimation function is called `feols()`. As a bare minimum, you need to pass a `pandas` or `polars` data frame 
and a Wilkinson formula.  

We first estimate a naive OLS regression that is likely biased by endogeneity (unobserved ability). Then we estimate a twin fixed-effects model. In both models, we cluster standard errors at the twin-pair level.

```{python}
fit_naive = pf.feols("log_wage ~ educ + experience", data=twins, vcov={"CRV1": "twin_pair_id"})
fit_fe = pf.feols("log_wage ~ educ + experience | twin_pair_id", data=twins, vcov={"CRV1": "twin_pair_id"})
```

We compare both specifications side by side via `etable()`.

```{python}
pf.etable(
    [fit_naive, fit_fe],
    labels={"log_wage": "Log Hourly Wage", "educ": "Years of Education", "experience": "Experience"},
    felabels={"twin_pair_id": "Twin Pair FE"},
    caption="Returns to Education: Naive OLS vs Twin Fixed Effects",
)
```

## Other relevant Features

After the initial regression, we recommend jumping directly to focused tutorials:

- [Formula Syntax](tutorials/formula-syntax.qmd): one-, two-, and three-part formulas, `i()`, and multiple-estimation operators.
- [Standard Errors & Inference](tutorials/standard-errors.qmd): heteroskedastic, clustered, and resampling-based inference.
- [Instrumental Variables](tutorials/instrumental-variables.qmd): IV estimation and diagnostics.
- [Poisson & GLMs](tutorials/poisson-glm.qmd): count-data models and generalized linear models.
- [Quantile Regression](tutorials/quantile-regression.qmd): distributional effects beyond the mean.

## Where to Go Next

| Tutorial | Description |
|---|---|
| [Formula Syntax](tutorials/formula-syntax.qmd) | Full formula language: FE, IV, `C()`, `i()`, and multiple-estimation syntax |
| [OLS with Fixed Effects](tutorials/ols-fixed-effects.qmd) | Twin studies, worker-firm panels, demeaning intuition |
| [Standard Errors & Inference](tutorials/standard-errors.qmd) | HC, cluster-robust, wild bootstrap, randomization inference |
| [Instrumental Variables](tutorials/instrumental-variables.qmd) | IV estimation, diagnostics, first-stage F-statistics |
| [Poisson & GLMs](tutorials/poisson-glm.qmd) | Count data models, logit, probit |
| [Multiple Estimation](tutorials/multiple-estimation.qmd) | `sw`, `csw`, split samples, multi-dep-var syntax |
| [Regression Tables](tutorials/regression-tables.qmd) | Publication-ready tables with Great Tables |
| [Difference-in-Differences](tutorials/difference-in-differences.qmd) | TWFE, Gardner's DID2S, event studies |
| [Quantile Regression](tutorials/quantile-regression.qmd) | Interior-point quantile regression |

You can browse all tutorials in the [Tutorial Gallery](tutorials/index.qmd), or see the [How-To Guides](how-to/marginaleffects.qmd) for task-oriented recipes. The [Function Reference](reference/index.qmd) has all details around functions and their arguments, classes, methods, and attributes.
